sections.heading: Traditional methods fell while overall use stayed steady
sections.body: Among currently married women, overall contraceptive use barely changed: 58.6 percent in 2025, compared with 58.3 percent in 2022. Traditional methods moved the other way, dropping from 16.5 percent to 14.1 percent. Withdrawal was still the main traditional method, but it also fell from 13.4 percent to 11.8 percent. Nationally, this does not show a broad move away from contraception. It shows traditional methods taking up a smaller share while total use stayed nearly the same.

sections.heading: Traditional-method use was highest in Ilocos Region and Eastern Visayas
sections.body: Among currently married women, traditional-method use was highest in Ilocos Region at 22.3 percent. Eastern Visayas followed at 20.4 percent, and CALABARZON was next at 18.1 percent. Central Luzon and Bicol Region were also above the national level, both at 17.2 percent. These higher-use regions are not all next to one another, but they are places where withdrawal remains a large part of the method mix. In Ilocos Region, withdrawal alone accounted for 20.2 percent.

sections.heading: Some regions had much sharper drops
sections.body: The national decline was modest, but several regions changed much more. BARMM fell from 14.7 percent in 2022 to 5.1 percent in 2025. Bicol Region dropped from 25.7 percent to 17.2 percent, and Central Visayas fell from 16.3 percent to 8.4 percent. MIMAROPA also declined from 14.5 percent to 8.6 percent. These changes suggest that a few large regional drops are driving the traditional-method trend, rather than an even shift across the country.

sections.heading: Most low-use regions were in Mindanao or Central Visayas
sections.body: In 2025, the lowest levels of traditional-method use were in BARMM at 5.1 percent, Cagayan Valley at 6.9 percent, Central Visayas at 8.4 percent, MIMAROPA at 8.6 percent, and Negros Island Region at 9.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was also below the national level at 9.4 percent. Several Mindanao regions were below 12 percent, including Northern Mindanao, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga. Davao Region was also below the national level at 12.2 percent. Across regions, the range ran from 5.1 percent to 22.3 percent, so location matters more than the small national change suggests.

sections.heading: Age differences were real, but not very large
sections.body: Among currently married women, traditional-method use was lowest at ages 15 to 19, at 8.7 percent. It was highest at ages 35 to 39, at 15.4 percent. Most adult age groups from 20 to 49 were in a fairly narrow range, from 12.1 percent to 15.4 percent. The clearer age pattern is that younger married women rely less on traditional methods than women in their late twenties through forties. Withdrawal makes up most of the traditional-method share in every age group.

sections.heading: Traditional methods were highest among women with one to two children
sections.body: By number of living children, traditional-method use was highest among currently married women with one to two children, at 16.7 percent. It was lower among women with three to four children at 11.9 percent, and nearly the same among women with five or more children at 12.0 percent. Women with no living children were also lower, at 9.0 percent. This pattern differs from total contraceptive use, which was highest among women with three to four children at 70.9 percent.

sections.heading: Urban married women used traditional methods more than rural married women
sections.body: Among currently married women, traditional-method use was higher in urban areas than in rural areas, at 15.8 percent versus 12.2 percent. The same table shows the opposite pattern for modern methods: rural women were at 47.2 percent, compared with 42.1 percent in urban areas. Since total contraceptive use was similar in rural and urban areas, this is not mainly about whether women used contraception at all. It is more about which methods they used.

sections.heading: Traditional use was higher among post-secondary and college groups
sections.body: Among currently married women, traditional-method use was highest for those with post-secondary education at 18.4 percent and college education at 16.6 percent. The post-secondary group rose sharply from 12.1 percent in 2022. The college group stayed high even though it declined from 19.0 percent. Lower-education groups did not show the same pattern: women with Grades 1 to 6 were at 9.3 percent, and women with no education were at 7.6 percent. This points to differences in method mix, not a simple education pattern in overall contraceptive use.

sections.heading: Traditional use was lowest in the poorest quintile, not the richest
sections.body: Across wealth quintiles, traditional-method use among currently married women was lowest in the lowest quintile, at 10.4 percent. The middle, fourth, and highest quintiles were all higher, at 16.0 percent, 15.0 percent, and 15.9 percent. The highest quintile did not have the highest overall contraceptive use, but it still had a relatively high share using traditional methods. Again, this points to differences in method mix rather than simple differences in access or overall use.

sections.heading: Sexually active unmarried women had a sharper drop
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, traditional-method use fell from 17.6 percent in 2022 to 11.7 percent in 2025. Withdrawal made up most of that level and most of the decline, dropping from 15.0 percent to 10.4 percent. Over the same period, modern-method use rose from 23.8 percent to 26.2 percent. This group is separate from currently married women, but it shows the same broad move away from traditional methods.

sections.heading: The residence gap narrowed for unmarried women, but rural use stayed higher
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, traditional-method use was higher in rural areas at 14.4 percent in 2025, compared with 10.3 percent in urban areas. Both areas declined sharply from 2022, when the figures were 20.8 percent for rural women and 16.0 percent for urban women. Withdrawal again made up nearly all of the traditional-method share in both places. The rural-urban gap remained, but the decline showed up in both urban and rural settings.

takeaways: Among currently married women, traditional-method use fell to 14.1 percent in 2025, even though total contraceptive use stayed nearly unchanged.
Regional differences are large: Ilocos Region and Eastern Visayas were high, while BARMM, Cagayan Valley, Central Visayas, and MIMAROPA were low.
Withdrawal is the main traditional method among both married women and sexually active unmarried women.
Sexually active unmarried women also moved away from traditional methods, with use falling from 17.6 percent in 2022 to 11.7 percent in 2025.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
